Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it never worked out that way. Most of my studying happened in bursts, usually late at night when I'd suddenly remember something was due. I have about 21 hours a week I could dedicate to studying, but it was scattered and unfocused. I'd often start something, get distracted, and then come back to it hours later without really knowing where I left off.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, man. It completely depended on what was breathing down my neck deadline-wise. Some weeks I wouldn't touch anything, and then suddenly I'd be cramming for multiple courses at once. With eight online courses, it was impossible to keep track of everything consistently. I'd react to deadlines rather than plan ahead, which I know isn't smart but that's just how it went.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being real. I'd get an email notification about a deadline or see someone in the discussion forum mention an assignment, and that would jolt me into logging in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important. It wasn't a habit—more like damage control whenever I remembered the course existed.
Q4: Not really, no. I only logged in maybe once a week on average, and it never felt automatic or routine. The closest it got was when I had a string of deadlines and I was checking in more frequently out of fear. But as soon as that pressure lifted, I'd drift away again. I never built that habit of just checking in regularly.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty helpful for me since I learn better by listening. I'd put them on and absorb the information better than reading dense text. The discussion posts were also useful because I could see how other people understood the material, which sometimes clicked things into place for me. I participated in discussions a decent amount because hearing different perspectives helped.
Q6: The reading materials and textbook links—I barely touched those. Reading just doesn't stick for me the same way listening does. I'd open them, skim a bit, and then close them feeling like I wasted my time. Some of the optional practice quizzes I ignored too because they felt like extra work when I was already drowning.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd do it. Activities that felt abstract or like busy work—I just couldn't make myself care. I needed to feel like the time I spent would actually pay off somehow. If someone explained why something mattered, I was more likely to engage with it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore. Required ones had consequences, so even if I didn't want to do them, the fear of failing pushed me. Optional stuff just got lost in the shuffle because I was already struggling to keep up with what was mandatory. It's not that I didn't think they'd be helpful—I just couldn't prioritize them.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing other stuff. Sometimes I'd take notes by hand because writing helps me process things, even though that doesn't show up anywhere. I also talked through concepts with friends outside of class, which was actually really helpful for understanding.
Q10: A lot of my learning happened through those audio files I'd downloaded and just replayed over and over. I'd be on the bus, at the gym, wherever, listening to lectures. None of that would show up in the LMS. Also, the peer interactions I had in person—study sessions, explaining things to each other—that was real learning but completely invisible online.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that was my copy. I'd go back to it multiple times offline, especially the audio lectures. The LMS would only show I accessed it once, but I might have listened to the same lecture three or four times trying to understand it. That single login doesn't capture the actual effort.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just crashed for me. I was scared—genuinely scared—about failing, but that fear paralyzed me instead of motivating me. I had too many courses demanding attention simultaneously, and I just shut down. The stress was medium-high, and I didn't have good coping mechanisms. I'd avoid the LMS because logging in meant confronting how behind I was.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue was a big one—I'd be exhausted and just couldn't focus on another online module. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and life stuff meant the LMS often got deprioritized. When I was confused about material, I'd avoid it rather than seek help, which made everything worse. The workload across eight courses was genuinely unmanageable.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the video playing but be completely zoned out, thinking about other things. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without actually reading them, just to feel like I did something. My mind would wander, and I'd realize I absorbed nothing. It looked like engagement but it was just going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of doing them badly made me avoid starting them at all. It's like this weird loop where the more scared I was about failing, the less I could bring myself to try. I knew it was self-sabotage but I couldn't break out of it. The CBT sessions I had helped me understand this pattern, but changing it was harder.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. But they also caused these intense stress spikes where I'd cram everything last minute. The LMS would show all my activity clustered right before due dates. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only system that worked for me, even if barely.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that course entirely. I'd take longer to respond to things after negative feedback because I was processing it emotionally. The feedback on my assignments was decent, but my exam performance was another story.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with eight courses running simultaneously. I couldn't keep track of which course needed what by when. A clearer, more spaced-out structure would have helped me stay on top of things. The way everything overlapped just made me feel like I was constantly failing at everything instead of succeeding at anything.
Q19: The discussion forums actually made engagement easier because I could interact with peers and that felt more human. But the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear prioritization made it harder. I never knew what was truly essential versus nice-to-have. More guidance on what to focus on would have helped someone like me who was struggling to keep up.
Q20: That was me, honestly. I'd be clicking around, watching videos, posting in discussions, but then I'd freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The assignments felt high-stakes and I was scared of being judged. Being active felt safer than committing to something that would be graded. It's easier to engage passively than to put yourself out there.
Q21: I can see how that happens if someone is really efficient or studies mostly offline. That wasn't exactly me—my low activity matched my low performance. But I know people who barely logged in but studied from downloaded materials and aced things. The LMS doesn't capture how smart or efficient someone is with their time outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just clicking through. They miss offline study, emotional state, life circumstances. The numbers tell a story, but it's not the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking buttons—it's being mentally present and invested. For me, engagement happened in moments, usually when something clicked or when I connected with classmates. It wasn't consistent, but when it happened, it was real.
Q24: The fear I felt—that wouldn't show up anywhere. The paralysis that came with being scared of failing, the way I'd avoid things precisely because they mattered to me. My CBT sessions helped me see these patterns, but the LMS just saw a student who didn't show up enough. It couldn't see that I wanted to succeed but was fighting against my own mind to do it.
